1: צַדִּיק (ṣaddîq) — "righteous." Noah is found righteous לְפָנַי (lĕpānay), "before me" — in the divine sight, not merely by human standards. The same positional phrase appears in Genesis 17:1 (Abram told to walk before God) and Genesis 48:15 (God before whom my fathers walked). Righteousness here is relational and covenantal rather than morally absolute — Noah stands upright in God's covenantal sight in a generation that does not. The Qal perfect rāʾîtî ("I have seen") presents this as God's already-completed assessment.
2: טָהוֹר (ṭāhôr) — "clean," and לֹא טָהוֹר (lōʾ ṭāhôr) — "not clean." The Levitical purity categories (Lev 11; Deut 14) are here anticipated without explanation — Noah already knows which animals are clean. The sevenfold and twofold distinction (seven pairs of clean, one pair of unclean) has sacrificial logic: Noah will offer burnt offerings from the clean animals (Genesis 8:20), requiring a surplus so the species survives the sacrifice. The text assumes pre-Sinaitic knowledge of clean/unclean categories.
3: The focus on "seven pairs" of birds mirrors the clean-animal provision of Genesis 7:2. לְחַיּוֹת זֶרַע (lĕḥayyôt zeraʿ) — "to keep alive seed/offspring upon the face of the earth" — the purpose clause frames the ark as a preservation vehicle rather than merely a survival vessel. The word zeraʿ (seed/offspring) will carry the promise in 3:15 and Genesis 12:7; here it is the mechanism of biological continuity across the flood.
4: אֶמְחֶה (ʾemḥeh) — "I will blot out/wipe away" — from māḥāh, the same verb used in Exodus 32:32-33 (blotting from the book of life) and Numbers 5:23 (blotting out the words of the curse). The verb is an erasure-word — the flood is presented as divine erasure of the corrupted creation. כָּל-הַיְקוּם (kol-hayyĕqûm) — "all the living substance/all that exists" — a comprehensive term covering all animate life created on the land.
5: וַיַּעַשׂ נֹחַ כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר-צִוָּהוּ יְהוָה — "and Noah did according to all that the LORD commanded him." The obedience formula closes the divine speech. This is the standard completeness formula used for tabernacle construction (Exodus 40:16: "Moses did everything just as the LORD commanded him") — Noah's compliance is total and unconditional, with no recorded question or objection.
6: בֶּן-שֵׁשׁ מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה — "son of six hundred years" — the Hebrew idiom for age uses ben (son of) with the number. The precise chronology beginning here (Genesis 7:6, 11) continues through Genesis 8:13-14 with specific month and day references — the flood narrative uses a calendrical precision absent from the creation account. The dating grounds the flood in real time and history rather than mythological timelessness.
7: מִפְּנֵי מֵי הַמַּבּוּל (mippĕnê mê hammabbûl) — "from before the waters of the flood." The preposition phrase mippĕnê (from before/because of) indicates motivation — they entered the ark in anticipation of and flight from the coming waters. Mabbûl (flood, deluge) is a specific technical term for this event; it appears only in Genesis 6-11 and Psalms 29:10 in the entire OT, suggesting it is a term of art for the Noahic flood specifically.
8: The verse restates the animal categories (clean/unclean, birds, all creeping things) to match the command of Genesis 7:2-3. The structural repetition (command → execution) is deliberate narrative technique throughout the Pentateuch — the tabernacle instructions and their execution (Exod 25-31; 35-40) follow the same pattern. The repetition signals total compliance and provides a legal/narrative completeness to the account.
9: שְׁנַיִם שְׁנַיִם (šĕnayim šĕnayim) — "two by two," the distributive construction by doubling the number. כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֱלֹהִים אֶת-נֹחַ — "as God commanded Noah." The shift from YHWH (Genesis 7:5) to ʾĕlōhîm here may reflect a different source perspective or may alternate naturally; in context both refer to the same God. The divine-command/animal-compliance motif continues: the animals come as if directed.
10: מֵי הַמַּבּוּל — "the waters of the flood." The waw-consecutive (wayyĕhî) marks the precise temporal fulfillment of the seven-day prediction in Genesis 7:4. The short verse is a hinge: it closes the preparation sequence and opens the flood sequence. The seven-day wait before the flood echoes the seven-day creation week — the flood begins on a seven-count, structurally parallel to creation's seven-day span.
11: נִבְקְעוּ כָּל-מַעְיְנֹת תְּהוֹם רַבָּה (nibqĕʿû kol-maʿyĕnōt tĕhôm rabbāh) — "all the fountains of the great deep burst open." Tĕhôm (the deep) is the word used in Genesis 1:2 — the primordial unformed deep over which God's spirit hovered. Its eruption here is the deliberate reversal of God's work in Genesis 1:6-9: the waters separated above and below in creation are now recombined. The flood is theologically un-creation — the separation established on days 2-3 is undone. אֲרֻבֹּת הַשָּׁמַיִם נִפְתָּחוּ — "the windows/floodgates of heaven were opened" — the upper waters above the firmament (Genesis 1:7) now pour through their openings. Both cosmic water-sources open simultaneously: the flood is as total as creation was total.
12: אַרְבָּעִים יוֹם וְאַרְבָּעִים לָיְלָה — "forty days and forty nights." The number forty is associated throughout the OT with periods of testing and transition: Moses on Sinai (Exodus 24:18; Genesis 34:28; Deuteronomy 9:9-25); Israel in the wilderness (Numbers 14:33-34; Deuteronomy 8:2-4); Elijah's journey to Horeb (1 Kings 19:8); Jesus's temptation (Matthew 4:2; par.). The forty-day rainfall is the initiating act; the waters will continue for 150 days (Genesis 7:24).
13: The verse reiterates the ark's entry on "that very day" (bĕʿeṣem hayyôm hazzeh) — the same phrase used of the Exodus departure from Egypt in Exodus 12:41 and the circumcision of Abraham in Genesis 17:23. The phrase marks singular historical events that happen at precisely the right time in God's plan. The naming of Noah's sons (Shem, Ham, Japheth) is here more than genealogical filler — they will structure the Table of Nations in Genesis 10.
14: לְמִינֵהוּ — "according to its kind" — the creation category from Genesis 1 (Genesis 1:11, 12, 21, 24, 25) recurs here. The ark preserves what creation established: the kinds remain distinct across the flood. The comprehensive listing (wild animals, livestock, creeping things, birds) mirrors the creation sequence of days 5-6 — the ark carries forward the entire creational taxonomy.
15: רוּחַ חַיִּים (rûaḥ ḥayyîm) — "breath of life." The same phrase used in Genesis 2:7 (God breathed the breath of life into Adam). Here it describes what all creatures that entered the ark possessed — the divine animating principle that makes them alive. The creatures saved are precisely those who bear this divine breath; all others will lose it in the flood.
16: וַיִּסְגֹּר יְהוָה בַּעֲדוֹ (wayyisgōr YHWH baʿăḏô) — "and the LORD shut him in." The verb sāgar (to shut, close) in the Qal: God personally seals the ark. This is the only divine action in the flood narrative that is not mediated through human or natural agency — God directly shuts the door. The personal closure signals total divine protection: what God seals cannot be opened by the waters outside. The preposition baʿăḏô (on his behalf, for him) carries a protective nuance — shut in for his safety.
17: The waw-consecutive sequence continues the rising flood narrative. וַיִּגְבְּהוּ (wayyigbĕhû) — "they rose/grew high" — the same root as gābôah (high, tall, exalted) used of God's height and mountains. The ark being lifted (wayyārām) on the increasing waters uses rûm (to rise, be lifted) — the same verb for exaltation elsewhere. The ark rises as the world is submerged — saved precisely by the waters that destroy.
18: וַתֵּלֶךְ הַתֵּבָה עַל-פְּנֵי הַמָּיִם (wattēlek hattēbāh ʿal-pĕnê hammāyim) — "and the ark moved on the surface of the waters." Hālaḵ (to walk/go) applied to the ark: it moves without human direction, propelled by the waters rather than navigated by crew. The phrase ʿal-pĕnê hammāyim (on the face/surface of the waters) repeats the construction of Genesis 1:2 (the Spirit hovered over pĕnê hammāyim) — the ark floats where the Spirit once hovered.
19: The emphatic triple intensification: מְאֹד מְאֹד (mĕʾōḏ mĕʾōḏ) — "very greatly" (double mĕʾōḏ, an intensification beyond what appears elsewhere in Genesis). All the high mountains under the whole heavens (kol-hehārîm haggĕbōhîm tǎḥat kol-haššāmāyim) — the sweep is deliberately total. The flood is geographically comprehensive in the text's own self-description.
20: חֲמֵשׁ עֶשְׂרֵה אַמָּה מִלְמַעְלָה גָּבְרוּ הַמָּיִם — "fifteen cubits upward the waters prevailed." A cubit is approximately 18 inches/45 cm — fifteen cubits = about 22 feet/7 meters above the highest mountains. The measurement may have a practical dimension: the ark's draft (how deep it sat in water) was probably about 15 cubits (half of the 30-cubit height in Genesis 6:15), so the water level at 15 cubits above the highest mountains means the ark barely clears them — a narrative detail that emphasizes the totality of the inundation while explaining why the ark was not grounded on mountaintops earlier.
21: וַיִּגְוַע (wayyigwaʿ) — "perished/died" — from gāwaʿ, to expire, breathe one's last. The same verb is used of the deaths of Ishmael (Genesis 25:17), Isaac (Genesis 35:29), Jacob (Genesis 49:33), and Aaron (Numbers 20:26, 28) — a dignified death verb, here applied at mass scale to the entire animal creation. The comprehensive list (birds, livestock, wild animals, swarming creatures, all people) mirrors the creation inventory of days 5-6 in reverse — creation is being unmade in the same order it was made.
22: כֹּל אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁמַת-רוּחַ חַיִּים בְּאַפָּיו (kōl ʾăšer nišmat-rûaḥ ḥayyîm bĕʾappāyw) — "all in whose nostrils was the breath (nišmāh) of the spirit of life." This combines both terms for divine animating breath: nišmāh (the breath of God breathed into Adam, Genesis 2:7) and rûaḥ ḥayyîm (spirit of life, Genesis 7:15). Those who die are precisely those who bore the God-given animating breath — the flood is the withdrawal of the divine life-principle from the land-creatures, leaving only those preserved in the ark.
23: וַיִּמַח (wayyimaḥ) — "and he blotted out/wiped out" — māḥāh, the verb God announced in Genesis 7:4. The divine announcement (Genesis 7:4) and its execution (Genesis 7:23) use the same word, closing the narrative bracket. וַיִּשָּׁאֶר אַךְ-נֹחַ (wayyiššāʾer ʾaḵ-nōaḥ) — "only Noah remained" — the ʾak (only, nevertheless) marks the sole exception in a universal erasure. The verse structure mirrors the beginning: God made, God unmade; only Noah remained to begin again.
24: חֲמִשִּׁים וּמְאַת יוֹם — "a hundred and fifty days." The waters prevailed (gābar, to be strong, prevail — the verb used of armies and power) for this duration before the wind of 8:1 reversed their course. The 150 days + the 40 days of rain = the full extent of the flood's height. The calendar precision (7:11 notes the 17th day of the 2nd month; Genesis 8:3-4 notes the 150 days ending with the ark resting on the 17th day of the 7th month) shows the author tracking the flood to the day.
